abstract: This report tracks shifts in language use and English proficiency among the U.S. Hispanic population from 1980 to 2013. Drawing on U.S. Census Bureau data, the analysis reveals a significant rise in English proficiency, driven primarily by a demographic shift toward U.S.-born Latinos and a slowdown in immigration from Latin America. While a record number of Hispanics continue to speak Spanish at home, the overall share of Spanish speakers has begun to decline as English becomes more prevalent across generations. Detailed demographic and economic profiles accompany the report, offering a comprehensive look at how these linguistic trends intersect with age, education, and nativity.
